title: Magret de canard
subtitle: Pan-seared duck breast with a glossy red-wine sauce
excerpt: Duck breast scored deeply through the fat, started in a cold pan, and rendered for eight minutes until the skin is mahogany and crisp. Rested for five minutes and served pink, with a quick pan sauce of shallots, red wine, and stock.
sourceNotes: Magret de canard refers specifically to the breast of a foie gras duck (mulard), which is larger and more flavourful than ordinary duck breast. The cooking method is documented in French gastronomy from the Gascony region, where foie gras production is centred. André Daguin of the Hôtel de France in Auch is credited with popularising the magret as a standalone cut in the 1960s.

content.text: The technique is the whole recipe. Duck breast has a thick layer of fat that must be rendered slowly in a cold pan before the heat goes up; start with a hot pan and the skin scorches before the fat underneath has had time to melt. Score the fat in a cross-hatch pattern down to (but not through) the meat, season generously, and lay the breast skin-side down in a dry, cold pan. Only then turn on the heat.

Duck is at its best served pink, an internal temperature of 57 to 60°C at the thickest point after resting. At 65°C it is still fine; beyond 70°C the flesh is dry and the fat has not rendered cleanly. The resting period is non-negotiable: five minutes at least, loosely tented with foil, before slicing diagonally.

items.name: Duck breast
items.prepNote: ~300 g each, skin scored in a cross-hatch

content.text: Score the fat of the {{duck-breast}} duck breasts deeply in a cross-hatch, 1 cm apart, cutting down to but not through the meat. Season both sides generously. Lay skin-side down in a cold, dry frying pan. Turn the heat to medium and cook without moving for 8 minutes, pouring off the fat as it accumulates. The skin should be deep brown and most of the fat layer rendered.

Turn the breasts over and cook meat-side down for 3 minutes for pink. Remove to a warm plate, tent loosely with foil, and rest for 5 minutes. Do not skip the rest, the temperature at the centre will rise by 3 to 5°C during resting and the juices will redistribute.

Pour off excess fat, leaving a thin film. Add the {{shallot}} to the pan and soften over medium heat for 2 minutes. Add the {{thyme}} and pour in the {{red-wine}} of red wine. Boil hard for 3 minutes until reduced by half. Add the {{stock-chicken}} of stock and simmer for 3 minutes more. Remove the thyme. Take the pan off the heat and whisk in the cold {{unsalted-butter}} of butter a piece at a time to emulsify the sauce. Slice the duck diagonally and serve with the sauce.

items.fix: Start in a cold pan, low-medium heat; the fat renders gradually and the skin crisps in its own fat
items.cause: Pan started too hot; fat basted the skin rather than rendering
items.symptom: Skin did not colour; duck is swimming in fat

items.fix: 3 minutes per side in a thin breast is plenty; the breast firms rapidly past 65°C
items.cause: Left too long on the meat side, or resting time skipped
items.symptom: Duck is cooked through with no pink

items.fix: Remove pan from heat entirely, let it cool for 30 seconds, then whisk in cold butter off the heat
items.cause: Butter added to a boiling pan, or pan too hot when butter was added
items.symptom: Pan sauce is separated and greasy

items.fix: Score deeply enough that the diamond pattern holds open during rendering; press the breast flat with a spatula in the first 2 minutes if needed
items.cause: Scoring too shallow; fat contracted and pulled the skin
items.symptom: Skin has rippled and contracted during cooking

content.text: Gascony, in the south-west of France, is duck country. The foie gras trade produces magret as a by-product. The breast of a force-fed duck is large and full of flavour. It is well-marbled with fat just under the skin in a way that ordinary duck breast is not. When André Daguin began cooking magret as a steak rather than as part of a confit, he borrowed a method from beef cookery: high heat, rare centre, quick rest, diagonal slice. The approach spread from Gascony into the wider French restaurant world. It became one of the signature dishes of French nouvelle cuisine in the 1970s.
